questions.question: The Triangle Shirtwaist Factory fire in 1911 killed 146 garment workers, mostly young immigrant women. Why was it so deadly?
options: The building collapsed in the fire
Managers had locked the exit doors to prevent theft and unauthorized breaks
The fire department had no ladders tall enough to reach upper floors (both true)
The fire spread through a gas pipeline
questions.answer: Managers had locked the exit doors to prevent theft and unauthorized breaks
questions.explanation: The Triangle Shirtwaist Factory occupied the top three floors of a building in Manhattan. When fire broke out, workers discovered exits had been locked by management to prevent theft and unauthorized breaks. 146 workers died — many jumping from windows to escape the flames. The tragedy galvanized the labor movement and led to major workplace safety legislation in New York that became a national model.
questions.memory_hook: Workers at Triangle died because managers locked the exits. Their deaths sparked America's labor safety movement — most modern workplace regulations trace back to this fire.

questions.question: Genghis Khan founded the Mongol Empire, which became the largest contiguous land empire in history. Approximately how much of the world's land area did it cover at its peak?
questions.explanation: The Mongol Empire at its peak (c.1270) covered about 24 million km² — approximately 16% of Earth's total land area, stretching from the Pacific Ocean to Eastern Europe. Genghis Khan (born Temüjin, c.1162) united the Mongol tribes in 1206 and conquered China, Persia, and parts of Europe through a combination of military tactics, speed, and psychological terror. His empire facilitated the Pax Mongolica — a period of trade and communication across Eurasia.
questions.memory_hook: The Mongol Empire covered 16% of Earth's land — the largest in history. It connected China to Europe for the first time, facilitating trade, plague, and the exchange of technology.

questions.question: The US Constitution was ratified in 1788, replacing which earlier governing document that had proven too weak?
questions.explanation: The Articles of Confederation (1781) created a very weak central government with no power to tax, no executive, no federal courts, and no ability to regulate commerce. Shays' Rebellion (1786-87) — a farmers' uprising that the federal government couldn't suppress — convinced leaders a stronger document was needed. The Constitutional Convention (1787) produced the current Constitution, ratified in 1788.
questions.memory_hook: The Articles of Confederation made a government so weak it couldn't even put down a farmers' revolt. The Constitution fixed this — but the debate over federal vs. state power has never fully ended.

questions.question: In tennis, the 'Golden Slam' is winning all four Grand Slams plus the Olympic gold in the same year. Who achieved this first?
questions.explanation: Steffi Graf of Germany achieved the Golden Slam in 1988 — the only player in tennis history to win all four Grand Slams (Australian Open, French Open, Wimbledon, US Open) and the Olympic gold medal in the same calendar year. She also won 22 Grand Slam singles titles in total, including a 6-year dominance of women's tennis. Her 1988 Wimbledon win didn't drop a single set.
questions.memory_hook: Steffi Graf's 1988 Golden Slam is unique in tennis history — no one else has won all four Grand Slams plus Olympic gold in one year. Her Wimbledon campaign that year didn't lose a set.

questions.question: The Stanley Cup, the oldest professional sports trophy in North America, has been awarded to the NHL champion since which year?
questions.explanation: Lord Stanley, Governor General of Canada, donated the Stanley Cup in 1892, and it was first awarded in 1893. The original cup is now preserved in the Hockey Hall of Fame; the trophy currently awarded is a replica rebuilt annually. The Montreal Canadiens have won it the most (24 times). The Cup is famous for players taking personal possession of it for a day during the summer — it has been to the Moon (in replica), North Pole, and many unusual locations.
questions.memory_hook: The Stanley Cup dates to 1893 — older than the NHL itself. Each winning player gets the Cup for a day in summer; it's been taken everywhere from strippers to the Pope's hands.

questions.question: Alfred Hitchcock's 1960 horror film 'Psycho' is famous for its shocking shower scene. What made it revolutionary for its time?
options: It featured the first CGI in film history
It killed off the main character very early, and depicted nudity and graphic violence unprecedented for mainstream cinema
It was the first film with a synchronized soundtrack
It was the first color horror film
questions.answer: It killed off the main character very early, and depicted nudity and graphic violence unprecedented for mainstream cinema
questions.explanation: Marion Crane (Janet Leigh), clearly set up as the protagonist, is killed in the shower at the Bates Motel only 30 minutes into Psycho. This subversion of audience expectations was unprecedented. Hitchcock showed toilet flushing (a first for mainstream American film) and implied nudity. He required theaters to not admit latecomers — also unprecedented. The film launched the slasher genre and transformed what was acceptable in mainstream cinema.
questions.memory_hook: Psycho killed its apparent main character 30 minutes in — audiences were in shock because protagonists never died early then. It launched the slasher genre and rewrote the rules of mainstream cinema.

questions.question: 'Bohemian Rhapsody' by Queen (1975) is one of the best-selling singles ever. Which band member wrote it?
questions.explanation: Freddie Mercury wrote 'Bohemian Rhapsody' — a 5-minute, 55-second operatic rock ballad that defied all commercial conventions. Radio DJs initially refused to play it because it was too long. It reached #1 in the UK for 9 weeks and has been covered, parodied, and featured in countless films. Following Mercury's death from AIDS in 1991, re-released for the AIDS charity, it became the UK's best-selling single of the 20th century.
questions.memory_hook: Freddie Mercury's 'Bohemian Rhapsody' was too strange and too long for radio — so the band insisted, and it hit #1 for 9 weeks. After Mercury died of AIDS in 1991, the re-release became the UK's best-selling single ever.

questions.question: Herman Melville's 'Moby-Dick' (1851) features Ahab's obsessive pursuit of a white sperm whale. What is the name of the ship's narrator who tells the story?
questions.explanation: The novel famously opens with 'Call me Ishmael' — one of the most recognizable opening lines in literature. Ishmael is the sole survivor of the Pequod's final encounter with the white whale. Melville's novel was a commercial failure on publication and nearly forgotten until the 20th century, when it was rediscovered as a masterpiece of American literature. Captain Ahab's obsession with Moby Dick has become a universal metaphor for destructive fixation.
questions.memory_hook: 'Call me Ishmael' is one of literature's great opening lines. Melville's novel flopped in 1851 and was nearly forgotten — it was rediscovered 70 years later as a masterpiece.

questions.question: The Niger River, West Africa's principal river, flows in an unusual boomerang shape. In which country does it originate — surprisingly close to the Atlantic Ocean — before flowing inland?
questions.explanation: The Niger River originates in the Fouta Djallon highlands of Guinea, just 240 km from the Atlantic coast. Rather than flowing directly to the sea, it curves northeast into the Sahara toward Mali, then bends southeast through Niger and Nigeria before finally emptying into the Gulf of Guinea via a massive delta. The 4,180 km journey is one of the world's great hydrological puzzles — a river that starts near the ocean, runs away from it, then returns.
questions.memory_hook: The Niger starts near the Atlantic in Guinea but runs inland into the Sahara before curving back 4,180 km later. Its backwards route confused European explorers for centuries.

questions.question: Patagonia, the vast windswept plateau at the southern tip of South America, is divided between which two countries?
questions.explanation: Patagonia encompasses the southern portions of both Argentina (east of the Andes) and Chile (west of the Andes), covering approximately 1 million km². It is famous for its extreme winds, glaciers, steppe grasslands, and wildlife including guanacos, penguins, condors, and southern right whales. Torres del Paine National Park in Chilean Patagonia is one of the world's most spectacular landscapes. Patagonia takes its name from 'Patagón' — Magellan's word for the indigenous people he met there.
questions.memory_hook: Patagonia straddles Argentina and Chile at South America's tip — a vast, windswept plateau of glaciers, steppe, and extraordinary wildlife. Magellan named it after the 'big-footed' people he encountered in 1520.

questions.question: Ocean currents play a critical role in regulating Earth's climate. What drives the 'thermohaline circulation' — the global conveyor belt of ocean currents?
options: Temperature and salinity differences that cause denser, saltier, colder water to sink, driving a global circulation pattern
questions.answer: Temperature and salinity differences that cause denser, saltier, colder water to sink, driving a global circulation pattern
questions.explanation: Thermohaline circulation is driven by density differences: cold salty water is denser and sinks, pulling warm surface water to replace it. In the North Atlantic, warm surface water flows north (Gulf Stream), releases heat to Europe, becomes colder and saltier, sinks, and flows south along the ocean floor. This cycle takes ~1,000 years to complete and keeps Western Europe ~5°C warmer than it would otherwise be. Climate change threatens to slow or collapse this system.
questions.memory_hook: The thermohaline conveyor belt keeps Western Europe 5°C warmer than it should be — without it, London would be as cold as Hudson Bay. Climate change threatens to slow or collapse it.

questions.question: Radioactive decay occurs when unstable atomic nuclei lose energy. What are the three main types of radioactive decay?
options: Alpha (helium nucleus), beta (electron), and gamma (electromagnetic radiation) decay
questions.answer: Alpha (helium nucleus), beta (electron), and gamma (electromagnetic radiation) decay
questions.explanation: Alpha decay emits a helium nucleus (2 protons + 2 neutrons), reducing atomic number by 2. Beta decay emits an electron (or positron), changing a neutron to a proton. Gamma decay emits high-energy electromagnetic radiation — the most penetrating. Alpha particles can be stopped by paper; beta by aluminum; gamma requires thick lead or concrete. Radioactive decay is the basis for nuclear dating, nuclear medicine (cancer treatment), and nuclear weapons.
questions.memory_hook: Alpha (stopped by paper), beta (stopped by aluminum), gamma (needs lead to stop). Each type changes the atom in different ways — alpha and beta change the element itself, gamma just releases energy.
